perturbation.original_text: As a condition to the Confidential Information being furnished to the Receiving Party and the directors, officers, partners, members, employees, agents, consultants, related investment funds, advisors, attorneys, accountants, affiliates, potential sources of capital and financing, financial advisors and other persons with whom the Receiving Party plans to work with respect to a potential cooperation (only those who receive the Confidential Information and are acting on Recipient’s behalf or in conjunction with the Recipient with respect to a potential cooperation possibility, collectively, “Representatives” of the Receiving Party), the Receiving Party agrees to treat the Confidential Information in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: As a condition to the Confidential Information being furnished to the Receiving Party and the directors, officers, partners, members, employees, agents, consultants, related investment funds, advisors, attorneys, accountants, affiliates, potential sources of capital and financing, financial advisors and other persons with whom the Receiving Party plans to work with respect to a potential cooperation (only those who receive the Confidential Information and are acting on Recipient’s behalf or in conjunction with the Recipient with respect to a potential cooperation possibility, collectively, “Associates” of the Receiving Party), the Receiving Party agrees to treat the Confidential Information in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Representatives' is defined clearly as those who receive confidential information and act on the Recipient's (Receiving Party) behalf. Changing it to 'Associates' introduces ambiguity. Are Associates the same as Representatives? This can create uncertainty in liability. This in-text contradiction is located on the fact that the the change of term causes ambiguity about who is liable if there is a breach of contract due to the misuse of the confidential information, who is defined as Representantives, since now they are Associates.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Limitations on Use and Disclosure of Confidential Information. (a) The Receiving Party shall, and shall direct its Representatives to, keep the Confidential Information in confidence and shall not disclose any of the Confidential Information in any manner whatsoever; provided, however, that (i) the Receiving Party may make any disclosure of information contained in the Confidential Information to which the Company gives its prior written consent, and (ii) any information contained in the Confidential Information may be disclosed to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who reasonably require access to such information for the purpose of evaluating a possible cooperation and who agree to keep such information in confidence to the same extent as described herein. The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or the breach of the terms of this Agreement applicable to Representatives by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Representatives' as those acting on the Receiving Party's behalf regarding cooperation and who receive confidential information. The changed text replaces 'Representatives' with 'Associates'. The contradicted text explicitly states the 'Receiving Party shall direct its Representatives to keep the Confidential Information in confidence' and 'The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement applicable to Representatives by any of its Representatives.' By changing Representatives to Associates, it introduces ambiguity and uncertainty regarding who is liable for a breach, directly contradicting the clause assigning responsibility for the actions of Representatives.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or the breach of the terms of this Agreement applicable to Representatives by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by the Receiving Party or the violation of the terms of this Agreement applicable to Representatives by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "breach" to "violation" creates an in-text contradiction. While seemingly similar, in legal contexts, 'breach' typically refers to a failure to perform a contractual obligation, whereas 'violation' can encompass a broader range of non-compliance, including statutory or regulatory infractions. Since 'breach' of contract is different than 'violation', since 'violation' can mean breaching the law, this creates an in-text contradiction regarding the responsibility and causes uncertainty in enforcement. Does the responsibility only account for the contractual obligations, or does it go beyond that?
perturbation.contradicted_text: Remedies . Each party acknowledges that in the event of any breach of the terms of this Agreement, the other party may not be made whole by monetary damages only. Accordingly, each party, in addition to any other remedy to which it may be entitled in law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek, at its sole expense, an injunction (which shall include a temporary restraining order) to prevent breaches of the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from "breach" to "violation" introduces a potential contradiction. The original text refers to a "breach of the terms of this Agreement," which aligns with the contradicted text about "breach of the terms of this Agreement." The modified text broadens the scope to "violation of the terms of this Agreement," which, according to the Perturbation Explanation, can encompass non-contractual infractions such as statutory or regulatory violations. This broader definition of 'violation' creates uncertainty, as it is unclear whether the 'Remedies' section applies only to contractual breaches or extends to these broader 'violations,' thus creating a strong in-text contradiction and uncertainty in enforcement, therefore, the answer is YES.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will have the exclusive authority to decide what Confidential Information (if any) is to be made available to the Receiving Party and its Representatives, provided, that the Receiving Party may decline to receive Confidential Information at any time for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will have the exclusive authority to decide what Sensitive Information (if any) is to be made available to the Receiving Party and its Representatives, provided, that the Receiving Party may decline to receive Sensitive Information at any time for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' in this context creates an in-text contradiction. 'Confidential Information' is a defined term in Section 1(b) which outlines exactly what it includes. 'Sensitive Information' is undefined and could reasonably be interpreted more broadly or narrowly. This contradiction affects the Company's obligation to provide specific information and the Recipient's right to decline it, as it can be interpreted that only the Sensitive Information that is not Confidential, can be declined and can create conflict and uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) The term “Confidential Information” shall include, without limitation, any and all information concerning the Company and its business, operations, strategy or prospects that is furnished to the Receiving Party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the Company and identified as confidential, whether furnished on or after the date of this Agreement, including, without limitation, any written analyses, business or strategic plans, compilations, studies, data, reports, interpretations, projections, forecasts, 1 records, notes, copies, excerpts, memoranda, documents or other materials (in whatever form maintained or conveyed, whether documentary, computerized form or otherwise), that contain or otherwise reflect information concerning the Company or its business, operations, strategy or prospects prepared by or on behalf of the Receiving Party or any of the Receiving Party’s Representatives or that otherwise reflect any conversations with Company Representatives (as defined below) describing or relating thereto. For any information transmitted orally to be deemed “Confidential Information”, it must be memorialized in writing, identified as confidential and provided to the Receiving Party in written form within five days of its disclosure to the Receiving Party. The Company acknowledges and agrees that neither it nor any of its Representatives have provided nonpublic information with respect to the Company to the Receiving Party prior to the execution and delivery of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information', which is a defined term in Section 1(b). The changed text replaces this with 'Sensitive Information', which is undefined. This creates a contradiction because the parties' obligations and rights regarding the provision and refusal of information now depend on a term that has no clear meaning, while a specific definition is available in the Contradicted Text. The definition of 'Confidential Information' becomes irrelevant, because of 'Sensitive Information' which opens to interpretation what can be declined or not. This creates a strong in-text contradiction based on the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: Without the Company’s prior written consent, which may be withheld by the Company in its sole discretion, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall direct its Representatives not to) initiate (other than through the Company’s financial and legal advisors, as designated by the Company in writing and other than contacts made or initiated in the ordinary course of business) any (a) communication concerning the Confidential Information, (b) requests for meetings with management of the Company in connection with the possible cooperation between the parties or (c) communication relating to the business of the Company or its affiliates or the possible cooperation, in each case, with any officer, director or employee of the Company or any of its affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: Without the Company’s prior written consent, which may be withheld by the Company in its sole discretion, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall direct its Representatives not to) initiate (other than through the Company’s financial and legal advisors, as designated by the Company in writing and other than contacts made or initiated outside the normal business activities) any (a) communication concerning the Confidential Information, (b) requests for meetings with management of the Company in connection with the possible cooperation between the parties or (c) communication relating to the business of the Company or its affiliates or the possible cooperation, in each case, with any officer, director or employee of the Company or any of its affiliates.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'ordinary course of business' to 'outside the normal business activities' introduces uncertainty. The original phrase is a commonly understood legal term. The new phrase is ambiguous. It is unclear what constitutes 'outside the normal business activities' and creates an in-text contradiction. What activities are acceptable and within normal business activities? This introduces vagueness regarding the permissibility of communications.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Communications . Without the Company’s prior written consent, which may be withheld by the Company in its sole discretion, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall direct its Representatives not to) initiate (other than through the Company’s financial and legal advisors, as designated by the Company in writing and other than contacts made or initiated outside the normal business activities) any (a) communication concerning the Confidential Information, (b) requests for meetings with management of the Company in connection with the possible cooperation between the parties or (c) communication relating to the business of the Company or its affiliates or the possible cooperation, in each case, with any officer, director or employee of the Company or any of its affiliates. The foregoing shall not apply to communication with the Chief Executive Officer of the Company Bill Carey.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'ordinary course of business' to 'outside the normal business activities' introduces ambiguity into the agreement. The original text uses a common legal term. The changed phrase is open to interpretation, and the contradictory text repeats the Changed Text. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because it's unclear what is allowed, specifically related to communication with the Company's CEO, Bill Carey. It contradicts the intention that the communication with the CEO is not impacted by restrictions on other communications. Therefore, it is a strong in-text contradiction.
